label: Spring Boot
tagline: Convention-over-configuration for production-ready Spring applications

content.overview: Spring Boot is an opinionated framework that auto-configures Spring application context based on the dependencies on your classpath. It bundles an embedded server (Tomcat/Undertow) and provides production-ready features via Actuator.

label: Core Spring
tagline: IoC container, dependency injection, and the application context

content.overview: Core Spring provides the Inversion of Control container that manages your application's objects (beans). Instead of creating dependencies yourself, you declare them and Spring injects them. This decouples components and makes them independently testable.
keyInsights: IoC inverts the control of object creation from the class to the framework
ApplicationContext is the advanced IoC container — BeanFactory is its leaner parent
Spring uses three forms of DI: constructor (preferred), setter, and field (@Autowired)

label: IoC Container
tagline: How Spring creates, configures, and wires application objects

content.overview: The Spring IoC container reads bean definitions (from @Configuration classes, component scanning, or XML), creates the objects, resolves dependencies, and manages their lifecycle. The ApplicationContext is the central interface.

sections.heading: Bean Definition Sources
sections.body: @Configuration classes with @Bean methods, @Component/@Service/@Repository/@Controller scanned classes, and (legacy) XML files. Spring Boot auto-configuration adds beans conditionally based on classpath contents.

sections.heading: Component Scanning
sections.body: @ComponentScan (implied by @SpringBootApplication) scans the package and sub-packages for @Component-annotated classes. @Service, @Repository, @Controller are @Component stereotypes that add semantic meaning and enable AOP advice (e.g., @Repository translates data access exceptions).

keyInsights: The container creates beans once by default (singleton scope) — stateless beans are thread-safe by design
@SpringBootApplication combines @Configuration + @EnableAutoConfiguration + @ComponentScan
Spring uses CGLIB to subclass @Configuration classes, making @Bean methods return the same singleton on repeated calls

label: Dependency Injection
tagline: Constructor, setter, and field injection — and which to prefer

content.overview: Dependency Injection (DI) is the pattern where an object's dependencies are provided externally rather than created inside. Spring's IoC container is the injector. Three injection styles exist in Spring: constructor, setter, and field.

sections.heading: Constructor Injection (Preferred)
sections.body: Dependencies are declared as constructor parameters. Since Spring 4.3, @Autowired is optional on single-constructor classes. Constructor injection makes dependencies explicit, enables immutable fields (final), and makes the class testable without a Spring context.

sections.heading: Field Injection (Avoid in Production)
sections.body: @Autowired directly on a field. Works but prevents making the field final, hides dependencies, and requires the Spring container to instantiate the class (can't unit test without it). Lombok's @RequiredArgsConstructor generates the constructor equivalent.

keyInsights: Constructor injection is mandatory for circular dependency detection — Spring throws on startup
@Qualifier("primary") or @Primary resolves ambiguity when multiple beans of the same type exist
@Value("${app.timeout:5000}") injects property values; supports SpEL expressions

label: Bean Lifecycle
tagline: From instantiation to destruction — what happens inside the container

content.overview: Spring manages the complete lifecycle of a bean: creation, dependency injection, initialization callbacks, ready state, and destruction callbacks. Understanding this lifecycle is essential for integrating with resources that need setup/teardown.

sections.heading: Lifecycle Phases
sections.body: 1. Instantiation (constructor called). 2. Dependency injection. 3. BeanNameAware.setBeanName(). 4. BeanPostProcessor.postProcessBeforeInitialization() — all registered BPPs run. 5. @PostConstruct method. 6. InitializingBean.afterPropertiesSet(). 7. @Bean(initMethod). 8. BeanPostProcessor.postProcessAfterInitialization() — AOP proxies created here. 9. Bean is ready. On shutdown: @PreDestroy → DisposableBean.destroy() → @Bean(destroyMethod).

sections.heading: BeanPostProcessor
sections.body: One of Spring's most powerful extension points. Runs after each bean is initialized. Spring itself uses BPPs for: @Autowired injection (AutowiredAnnotationBeanPostProcessor), @Async, @Transactional, AOP proxy creation (AnnotationAwareAspectJAutoProxyCreator).

sections.heading: Scopes
sections.body: Singleton (default): one instance per container. Prototype: new instance per injection/getBean() call. Request/Session: one instance per HTTP request/session (web contexts). Custom scopes possible. Prototype beans injected into singletons freeze at injection time — use ObjectProvider<T> or scoped proxy to get fresh instances.

keyInsights: AOP proxies are created in BeanPostProcessor.postProcessAfterInitialization() — self-invocation bypasses them
@PostConstruct is the right place to validate configuration — throw early rather than fail at runtime
ApplicationContext.close() triggers @PreDestroy — register shutdown hook: context.registerShutdownHook()

label: ApplicationContext
tagline: The central Spring container — more than just a bean factory

content.overview: ApplicationContext extends BeanFactory with enterprise features: event publishing, internationalization (MessageSource), resource loading, AOP integration, and a parent-child context hierarchy. In Spring Boot, AnnotationConfigServletWebServerApplicationContext is the concrete type.

sections.heading: Context Events
sections.body: Spring publishes events at lifecycle milestones: ContextRefreshedEvent (all singletons initialized), ContextStartedEvent, ContextStoppedEvent, ContextClosedEvent. Listen with @EventListener or implement ApplicationListener<T>.

keyInsights: ApplicationContext.getBean() is service-locator anti-pattern — prefer constructor injection
SpringApplication.run() bootstraps the context, runs CommandLineRunner/ApplicationRunner beans, and starts the server
Lazy initialization (spring.main.lazy-initialization=true) defers bean creation to first use — speeds startup

label: Auto-Configuration
tagline: How Spring Boot configures itself from classpath contents

content.overview: Auto-configuration is Spring Boot's killer feature. It examines your classpath and registered beans, then conditionally creates beans to complete your application configuration. It's implemented via @Conditional annotations on @Configuration classes.
keyInsights: Auto-configuration backs off when you define your own beans — 'convention over configuration'
--debug flag shows the auto-configuration report: which configurations were applied and which were not
Exclude specific auto-configs with @SpringBootApplication(exclude = DataSourceAutoConfiguration.class)

label: How Auto-Config Works
tagline: From spring.factories to AutoConfiguration.imports

content.overview: Spring Boot discovers auto-configuration classes through META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports (Spring Boot 2.7+; previously META-INF/spring.factories). These classes use @Conditional annotations to decide whether to apply.

sections.heading: Discovery Mechanism
sections.body: SpringFactoriesLoader reads the imports file. Each entry is a fully-qualified @AutoConfiguration class. Spring Boot processes them in order (influenced by @AutoConfigureBefore/@AutoConfigureAfter), applying only those whose conditions match.

keyInsights: Auto-configuration classes are loaded AFTER user beans — that's how @ConditionalOnMissingBean works
spring-boot-autoconfigure.jar contains all of Spring Boot's built-in auto-configurations — reading it is the best documentation
Auto-configuration ordering: @AutoConfigureBefore/@AutoConfigureAfter/@AutoConfigureOrder

label: Conditional Beans
tagline: @ConditionalOnClass, @ConditionalOnMissingBean, and friends

content.overview: @Conditional annotations make bean creation depend on environmental conditions. Spring Boot defines many: @ConditionalOnClass (classpath check), @ConditionalOnMissingBean (backoff), @ConditionalOnProperty (config value), @ConditionalOnWebApplication, and more.

sections.heading: Key Conditionals
sections.body: @ConditionalOnClass: create bean only if class is on classpath. @ConditionalOnMissingBean: create only if no bean of that type exists. @ConditionalOnProperty: based on application.properties value. @ConditionalOnExpression: SpEL expression. @ConditionalOnJava: JVM version.

keyInsights: Custom conditions: implement Condition interface and use @Conditional(MyCondition.class)
@ConditionalOnMissingBean is evaluated after all user @Configuration classes — ensures backoff works
Conditions have phases: PARSE_CONFIGURATION (early) and REGISTER_BEAN (late) — use the right phase

label: Custom Starter
tagline: Packaging reusable Spring Boot configuration as a library

content.overview: A Spring Boot starter is a Maven/Gradle dependency that brings auto-configuration + related dependencies. Creating a custom starter lets you share infrastructure configuration across projects: include it as a dependency and it auto-wires itself.

sections.heading: Starter Structure
sections.body: Two modules: autoconfigure module (the auto-configuration logic) and starter module (just a POM that depends on autoconfigure + any libraries). Name convention: acme-spring-boot-autoconfigure and acme-spring-boot-starter.

keyInsights: Use @ConfigurationProperties for type-safe configuration binding — get IDE autocomplete in application.properties
Add spring-boot-configuration-processor to generate metadata for IDE hints
Never @ComponentScan in autoconfigure modules — it conflicts with user component scanning

label: Spring MVC
tagline: The Servlet-based web framework for building REST APIs

content.overview: Spring MVC is the Servlet-based web framework in Spring. It handles HTTP requests through a pipeline: DispatcherServlet receives all requests, delegates to HandlerMapping to find the right @Controller, invokes it through HandlerAdapter, and renders the response.
keyInsights: Spring MVC is thread-per-request — each HTTP request occupies a thread from the Tomcat thread pool
@RestController = @Controller + @ResponseBody on every method
Content negotiation: Spring picks response format based on Accept header and registered converters

label: Request Lifecycle
tagline: From HTTP packet to @Controller and back

content.overview: Every HTTP request in Spring MVC follows the same pipeline: it enters through the Servlet container (Tomcat), passes through the filter chain, reaches DispatcherServlet, which orchestrates handler resolution, method invocation, and response writing.

sections.heading: DispatcherServlet Pipeline
sections.body: 1. Request arrives at Tomcat → passes through Filter chain (Spring Security filters, CORS, logging). 2. DispatcherServlet.doDispatch() is called. 3. HandlerMapping finds the handler (your @Controller method) and any HandlerInterceptors. 4. HandlerAdapter invokes the method via reflection, processing @RequestBody, @PathVariable, @RequestParam. 5. Return value processed by HttpMessageConverter (e.g., Jackson to JSON). 6. Response written.

sections.heading: HandlerInterceptors vs Filters
sections.body: Filters (javax.servlet.Filter) operate at the Servlet level — they see the raw request before DispatcherServlet. HandlerInterceptors operate inside Spring MVC — they can access handler information (preHandle, postHandle, afterCompletion). Use Filters for auth/logging; interceptors for MVC-specific cross-cutting concerns.

keyInsights: DispatcherServlet is a singleton — all requests go through one instance; it's stateless and thread-safe
@RequestBody deserialization errors throw HttpMessageNotReadableException — handle with @ControllerAdvice
HandlerMethodArgumentResolver is the extension point for custom method parameter types

label: REST Controllers
tagline: Building RESTful APIs with @RestController

content.overview: @RestController combines @Controller and @ResponseBody, making every method's return value serialized directly to the HTTP response. Combined with Spring's HttpMessageConverters (Jackson for JSON), it enables building REST APIs with minimal boilerplate.

sections.heading: Common Annotations
sections.body: @GetMapping/@PostMapping/@PutMapping/@DeleteMapping/@PatchMapping (shortcuts for @RequestMapping with method). @PathVariable (URI segments), @RequestParam (query string), @RequestBody (deserialize JSON body), @RequestHeader, @CookieValue. @ResponseStatus sets the HTTP status code.

sections.heading: Validation
sections.body: @Valid or @Validated on @RequestBody enables Bean Validation (Jakarta Validation / Hibernate Validator). Constraint annotations: @NotNull, @NotBlank, @Size, @Email, @Pattern, @Min, @Max. Validation failures throw MethodArgumentNotValidException — catch with @ControllerAdvice.

keyInsights: ResponseEntity<T> gives full control over status, headers, and body
Use @JsonView or DTOs to control serialization — never expose entity objects directly
ProblemDetail (RFC 9457, Spring 6+) is the standard error response format — Spring Boot uses it by default

label: Exception Handling
tagline: @ControllerAdvice and the centralized error response pattern

content.overview: @ControllerAdvice is a specialization of @Component that applies globally to all controllers. It's used to centralize exception handling (@ExceptionHandler), model attribute binding (@ModelAttribute), and data binding init (@InitBinder).

sections.heading: @ExceptionHandler
sections.body: Methods annotated with @ExceptionHandler handle exceptions thrown from any controller. The method signature is flexible — Spring inspects it to provide arguments (the exception, HttpServletRequest, HttpStatus, etc.).

keyInsights: @RestControllerAdvice = @ControllerAdvice + @ResponseBody — use for REST APIs
Spring 6 / Boot 3 automatically returns ProblemDetail (RFC 9457) for common errors when configured
Handler exception resolvers: HandlerExceptionResolver → @ExceptionHandler → ResponseEntityExceptionHandler

label: Filters & Interceptors
tagline: Cross-cutting concerns in the request processing chain

content.overview: Filters (Servlet level) and HandlerInterceptors (Spring MVC level) both intercept requests but at different points in the pipeline. Filters see the raw Servlet request; interceptors run inside Spring MVC and can access handler metadata.

sections.heading: OncePerRequestFilter
sections.body: Spring's base class for Servlet filters. Guarantees once-per-request execution (Servlet spec allows filters to run multiple times in forward/include). Override doFilterInternal(request, response, chain).

keyInsights: Filter ordering: FilterRegistrationBean.setOrder() or @Order on the filter class
HandlerInterceptor.preHandle returning false stops the chain — use for auth/rate-limiting
Spring Security is a chain of ~15 filters — they run before your app filters in a specific order

label: Spring Data
tagline: Repositories, JPA, and declarative data access

content.overview: Spring Data provides a consistent, repository-based abstraction over different data stores (JPA/Hibernate, MongoDB, Redis, Cassandra, Elasticsearch). JpaRepository gives you CRUD, paging, sorting, and derived query methods out of the box.
keyInsights: Spring Data generates repository implementations at startup — there's no .impl class to write
Entity Manager is not thread-safe — Spring wraps it in a proxy that delegates to the current transaction's EM
Lazy loading outside a transaction causes LazyInitializationException — a very common bug

label: JPA Overview
tagline: Object-Relational Mapping with Jakarta Persistence

content.overview: JPA (Jakarta Persistence API) is the standard ORM specification for Java. Hibernate is the most common implementation. JPA maps Java classes to database tables, manages object identity, and provides a query language (JPQL) and criteria API.

sections.heading: Entity Mapping
sections.body: @Entity marks a class as a JPA entity. @Id + @GeneratedValue for primary key. @Column, @Table for table/column names. Relationships: @OneToMany, @ManyToOne, @OneToOne, @ManyToMany. FetchType.LAZY is the default for collections (and should be for @ManyToOne too).

sections.heading: N+1 Problem
sections.body: When you load N entities and access a lazy association on each, JPA fires N additional queries. Solution: use JOIN FETCH in JPQL, @EntityGraph, or @BatchSize to load associations efficiently.

keyInsights: Use FetchType.LAZY everywhere — only eagerly fetch when you know you always need the association
equals/hashCode on JPA entities: use business key, not auto-generated id (id is null before persist)
spring.jpa.show-sql=true + format_sql reveals all executed SQL — essential for finding N+1 in development

label: Repositories
tagline: CrudRepository, JpaRepository, and derived query methods

content.overview: Spring Data repositories are interfaces you declare; Spring generates the implementation. JpaRepository extends PagingAndSortingRepository extends CrudRepository. You add methods by following naming conventions or writing JPQL/native SQL with @Query.

sections.heading: Derived Query Methods
sections.body: Spring Data parses method names to generate queries. Supports: findBy, countBy, existsBy, deleteBy. Conditions: Equals (default), Like, StartingWith, Between, LessThan, In, IsNull, OrderBy.

keyInsights: @Modifying queries need @Transactional on the calling service method
Projections (interfaces or DTOs) let you SELECT only needed columns — avoid loading full entities for list views
Specifications (JPA Criteria API) for dynamic queries — combine with Predicate building for filter UI

label: @Transactional
tagline: Declarative transaction management and propagation behaviors

content.overview: @Transactional wraps a method in a database transaction. Spring uses AOP to intercept the method, begin a transaction before, commit after successful return, or rollback on RuntimeException/Error.

sections.heading: Propagation Behaviors
sections.body: REQUIRED (default): join existing transaction or create new. REQUIRES_NEW: always create new, suspend existing. NESTED: savepoint within existing. SUPPORTS: run in transaction if exists, non-transactional if not. NEVER: throw if transaction exists.

sections.heading: Self-Invocation Trap
sections.body: @Transactional works via AOP proxy. Calling a @Transactional method from within the same class bypasses the proxy — no transaction is started. Solution: inject self (Application context), use AspectJ weaving, or restructure into two beans.

keyInsights: @Transactional(readOnly=true) hints Hibernate to skip dirty checking — use for all read-only operations
Default rollback: only RuntimeException and Error — checked exceptions do NOT roll back by default
Transaction isolation: READ_COMMITTED is usually the right default; avoid SERIALIZABLE except for critical sections

label: Query Methods
tagline: JPQL, native SQL, and Querydsl for complex queries

content.overview: Beyond derived query methods, Spring Data supports @Query for JPQL/native SQL, Querydsl for type-safe dynamic queries, and JPA Specifications for composable filter predicates.

sections.heading: @Query with Named Parameters
sections.body: Write JPQL or native SQL directly. Named parameters (:name) or positional (?1). nativeQuery=true for database-specific SQL.

keyInsights: JPQL operates on entities and their mapped fields — not table/column names
Spring Data JPA Projections: interface-based (Spring generates proxy) or class-based (DTO projection via constructor)
Pagination: return Page<T> and accept Pageable parameter — Spring adds LIMIT/OFFSET automatically

label: Spring Security
tagline: Authentication and authorization for Spring applications

content.overview: Spring Security is a powerful authentication and authorization framework built on a chain of Servlet filters. It handles login flows, session management, CSRF protection, OAuth2, and method-level security via AOP.
keyInsights: SecurityFilterChain is the modern config API (Spring Security 5.7+) — WebSecurityConfigurerAdapter is deprecated
Spring Security is opt-in but auto-configured by Spring Boot when on classpath — adds HTTP Basic auth by default
SecurityContextHolder stores the Authentication in a ThreadLocal — cleared after each request

label: Security Filter Chain
tagline: The ordered chain of filters that enforces Spring Security

content.overview: Spring Security is implemented as a chain of ~15 Servlet filters. Every HTTP request passes through all of them in order. Filters handle: channel security, concurrent session, security context, logout, authentication (Basic, Form, OAuth2), exception translation, and authorization.

sections.heading: Filter Order (abbreviated)
sections.body: SecurityContextPersistenceFilter (load SecurityContext) → LogoutFilter → UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter (form login) → BasicAuthenticationFilter → ExceptionTranslationFilter (converts exceptions to 401/403) → FilterSecurityInterceptor (authorization decision).

sections.heading: SecurityContext and Authentication
sections.body: After successful authentication, Spring Security creates an Authentication object (principal, credentials, authorities) and stores it in the SecurityContextHolder. Controllers can access it via @AuthenticationPrincipal or SecurityContextHolder.getContext().getAuthentication().

keyInsights: ExceptionTranslationFilter is key: AuthenticationException → 401, AccessDeniedException → 403 (or redirect to login)
JWT auth: add a custom OncePerRequestFilter before UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter to parse and validate the token
Method security: @EnableMethodSecurity + @PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN')") on service methods

label: Authentication Flow
tagline: How Spring Security verifies user identity

content.overview: Spring Security's authentication flow: an authentication filter extracts credentials from the request, creates an unauthenticated Authentication token, delegates to AuthenticationManager (usually ProviderManager), which tries each AuthenticationProvider until one succeeds.

sections.heading: ProviderManager and Providers
sections.body: ProviderManager iterates through configured AuthenticationProviders. DaoAuthenticationProvider (username/password): loads UserDetails via UserDetailsService, validates password with PasswordEncoder. JwtAuthenticationProvider (custom): validates JWT, extracts claims.

keyInsights: Always use PasswordEncoder (BCrypt, Argon2) — never store plaintext passwords
UserDetails.isAccountNonExpired, isAccountNonLocked, isCredentialsNonExpired — implement for account management
Remember-me: AbstractRememberMeServices — persistent token stored in database, not cookie value

label: Authorization
tagline: Controlling access to resources and methods

content.overview: Authorization in Spring Security decides whether an authenticated principal can access a resource. HTTP-level authorization uses request matchers in SecurityFilterChain. Method-level authorization uses @PreAuthorize, @PostAuthorize, @Secured.

sections.heading: Method Security
sections.body: @PreAuthorize runs before the method — can reject before any processing. @PostAuthorize runs after — can check return value. Expressions use Spring Security's SpEL (hasRole, hasAuthority, isAuthenticated, principal, #returnObject).

keyInsights: @PreAuthorize is AOP-based — self-invocation bypasses it (same gotcha as @Transactional)
OAuth2 Resource Server: spring-security-oauth2-resource-server + spring.security.oauth2.resourceserver.jwt.issuer-uri
GrantedAuthority vs Role: roles are authorities prefixed with ROLE_ — hasRole() adds the prefix automatically

label: Spring AOP
tagline: Aspect-Oriented Programming for cross-cutting concerns

content.overview: AOP (Aspect-Oriented Programming) separates cross-cutting concerns (logging, security, transactions, caching) from business logic. Spring AOP uses proxies — either JDK dynamic proxies (interface-based) or CGLIB subclass proxies.
keyInsights: Spring AOP is proxy-based — it only intercepts external method calls through the proxy
AspectJ (full AOP) does bytecode weaving and can intercept self-calls and private methods — more powerful but more complex
@Transactional, @Cacheable, @Async, @Secured are all implemented via Spring AOP

label: Proxy Mechanism
tagline: JDK proxies vs CGLIB and when Spring uses each

content.overview: Spring creates a proxy around beans that need AOP advice. JDK dynamic proxies only work for interfaces. CGLIB creates a subclass of the target class and overrides methods. Spring Boot defaults to CGLIB to avoid the 'must use interface' restriction.

sections.heading: JDK Dynamic Proxy
sections.body: Created with java.lang.reflect.Proxy. The proxy implements the same interfaces as the target. Method calls are dispatched through InvocationHandler.invoke(). Only works if the target implements at least one interface. Slightly faster at call time once warmed.

sections.heading: CGLIB Proxy
sections.body: Creates a subclass: class UserService$$EnhancerBySpringCGLIB extends UserService. Overrides non-final methods to intercept calls. Works without interfaces. Spring Boot uses CGLIB by default since Spring 4 (proxyTargetClass=true). Final classes/methods cannot be proxied.

keyInsights: final class or final method = cannot be CGLIB proxied — @Transactional won't work
AopContext.currentProxy() lets a bean access its own proxy for self-invocation — use sparingly
Proxy creation happens in BeanPostProcessor.postProcessAfterInitialization() — before the bean is 'ready'

label: Advice Types
tagline: @Before, @After, @Around, @AfterReturning, @AfterThrowing

content.overview: Advice is the action taken by an aspect at a join point. Spring AOP supports five advice types that cover before, after, and around a method execution.

sections.heading: Advice Annotations
sections.body: @Before: runs before the method. @AfterReturning: runs after successful return (can access return value). @AfterThrowing: runs if method throws (can access exception). @After: always runs (like finally). @Around: wraps the method call — most powerful, controls execution and can change return value.

keyInsights: @Around is the most commonly used advice — it gives full control including changing arguments and return value
Advice ordering: use @Order on the aspect class or implement Ordered interface
JoinPoint gives access to method signature, args, and target object in @Before/@After

label: Pointcut Expressions
tagline: Matching join points with AspectJ pointcut syntax

content.overview: Pointcuts define which join points (method executions) advice applies to. Spring AOP uses AspectJ's pointcut expression language. The most common designators are execution() (method signature) and @annotation() (annotation-based matching).

sections.heading: Expression Syntax
sections.body: execution(modifiers? return-type declaring-type? method-name(params) throws?). Wildcards: * (any sequence in name), .. (any params or any subpackage).

keyInsights: execution is the most commonly used designator — prefer it for performance over within/this/target
Named pointcuts (@Pointcut on a method) enable reuse across multiple advice methods
Spring AOP only supports method-level join points — for field access, use full AspectJ

label: Observability
tagline: Metrics, health checks, and distributed tracing

content.overview: Spring Boot Actuator provides production-ready observability features: health checks, metrics (via Micrometer), info endpoint, and HTTP trace. Micrometer is a vendor-neutral metrics facade that exports to Prometheus, Datadog, CloudWatch, and others.
keyInsights: Actuator endpoints are disabled by default in production config — expose only what you need
Micrometer Tracing (Spring Boot 3) integrates with Zipkin/Jaeger for distributed tracing
Spring Boot 3 natively instruments HTTP requests, JVM metrics, DataSource pool metrics via auto-configuration

label: Actuator
tagline: Health checks, info, and management endpoints for production

content.overview: Spring Boot Actuator adds /actuator/* endpoints for monitoring and managing your application. Key endpoints: /health (liveness/readiness), /metrics, /info, /env, /beans, /threaddump, /loggers, /prometheus.

sections.heading: Configuration
sections.body: Enable endpoints in application.properties. Security: expose only /health and /info publicly, protect others.

codeSnippet.code:
management:
  endpoints:
    web:
      exposure:
        include: health,info,metrics,prometheus
  endpoint:
    health:
      show-details: always
      probes:
        enabled: true  # /health/liveness and /health/readiness for Kubernetes
  metrics:
    tags:
      application: ${spring.application.name}

sections.heading: Custom Health Indicators
sections.body: Implement HealthIndicator to add custom health checks (external service availability, disk space, business invariants). The aggregate /health rolls up all indicators.

keyInsights: Kubernetes liveness probe = /health/liveness (is the process alive?), readiness = /health/readiness (ready to serve traffic?)
/actuator/loggers allows changing log levels at runtime without restart — invaluable for production debugging
/actuator/beans lists all beans and their dependencies — the ultimate dependency graph

label: Micrometer Metrics
tagline: Application metrics with Micrometer and Prometheus

content.overview: Micrometer is a metrics instrumentation library — the SLF4J of metrics. It provides a vendor-neutral API and adapters (registries) for Prometheus, Datadog, CloudWatch, Graphite, and more. Spring Boot auto-configures the MeterRegistry.

sections.heading: Metric Types
sections.body: Counter (monotonically increasing: requests served), Gauge (current value: active connections), Timer (duration + count: request latency), DistributionSummary (distribution of values: payload sizes). Tags add dimensions: app=codemap, region=us-east-1.

sections.heading: @Timed Annotation
sections.body: Annotate methods with @Timed (from io.micrometer.core.annotation) to automatically record method duration. Requires TimedAspect bean registered. Spring Boot auto-configures this.

keyInsights: Prometheus scrapes /actuator/prometheus — add spring-boot-starter-actuator + micrometer-registry-prometheus
Histogram percentiles vs precomputed percentiles: precomputed (client-side) can't be aggregated across instances
Use Tags for dimensions (environment, region, version) — avoid high-cardinality tags (user IDs, request IDs)
